title: Treacle tart with lattice top
subtitle: Golden syrup filling in a shortcrust case with a woven lattice
excerpt: A classic British treacle tart: golden syrup thickened with breadcrumbs and sharpened with lemon juice, in a shortcrust pastry case with a woven lattice top. The filling bakes to a sticky, caramel-sweet set.
sourceNotes: Treacle tart is a British confection documented from the late nineteenth century, coinciding with the commercial availability of golden syrup (Lyle's Golden Syrup launched 1883). Mrs Beeton, Book of Household Management (1861), Project Gutenberg #10136, covers syrup-based tart fillings; the golden syrup and breadcrumb combination appears in British domestic manuals from the 1890s onward. The lattice top is a traditional presentation found throughout late-Victorian and Edwardian baking literature.

recipe.makeAheadNotes: Treacle tart is good warm or at room temperature; it keeps covered for 2 days.

content.text: Golden syrup is very fluid when warm and sets firm as it cools; the breadcrumbs absorb the excess syrup during baking and give the filling its texture. Fresh white breadcrumbs from a soft loaf give a smoother result than dried. The lemon juice is not optional: without it the filling is cloyingly sweet. The lattice strips are cut from the pastry trimmings and laid across the filling before baking; they will puff and colour as the filling sets.
Make the pastry and line the tin

Rub the cold butter into the flour and icing sugar until the mixture resembles breadcrumbs. Add the egg yolk and cold water and mix until a dough forms. Flatten, wrap, and refrigerate for 30 minutes. Heat the oven to 180°C fan. Roll two thirds of the pastry on a lightly floured surface to 3 mm and use it to line a 23 cm tart tin, pressing into the edges and leaving some overhang. Refrigerate the lined tin and set aside the remaining pastry for the lattice.

Warm the golden syrup in a saucepan until just fluid. Remove from the heat and stir in the double cream and lemon juice. Stir in the breadcrumbs and leave for 2 minutes to absorb. Pour into the chilled pastry case. Roll the remaining pastry thinly and cut into strips about 1.5 cm wide. Lay strips across the filling in a lattice pattern, pressing the ends onto the pastry rim. Trim the overhang flush with a knife.

Bake for 35 to 40 minutes, until the pastry is golden and the filling is set with only a slight wobble in the very centre. Cool in the tin for 20 minutes before slicing. Serve warm with clotted cream or vanilla ice cream.
